recap: Factorising reverses expanding — find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b for x squared + bx + c, or take out the HCF for simpler expressions.

steps: Need two numbers that multiply to 20 and add to 9.
Need two numbers that multiply to -21 and add to -4.
Part (a): Difference of two squares. x squared - 64 = x squared - 8 squared.
Part (b): First take out HCF: 3x squared - 27 = 3(x squared - 9).
Then apply difference of two squares: 3(x + 3)(x - 3).

summary: Always look for a common factor first when factorising any expression.
For x squared + bx + c, find two numbers with product c and sum b.
Use the difference of two squares rule: a squared - b squared = (a + b)(a - b).
Check your factorisation by expanding the brackets to confirm you get the original expression.
